name: Question of the Day Circle -- Philosophical Inquiry Together
description: Start each session with a philosophical question: 'Is it ever okay to break a promise?' 'Can you be brave and scared at the same time?' 'What makes something real?' Everyone sits in a circle and takes turns responding, building on each other's ideas. The facilitator asks follow-up questions: 'Why do you think that? What would someone who disagrees say? Can you give an example?' The goal is not to find the answer but to think more deeply together.
learning_objectives: Practice asking and exploring open-ended philosophical questions
Build on others' ideas through respectful dialogue
Develop comfort with questions that have no single right answer
variations: Students generate and vote on the question
Pair-share before whole-group discussion
Written reflection after discussion

name: Wonder Wall -- Big Questions Board
description: Create a physical or digital board where learners post questions that have no easy answers. Questions like 'Why is there something instead of nothing?' 'Do animals think?' 'What is time?' are posted, read, and discussed. The Wonder Wall grows throughout the year. Periodically revisit old questions to see if thinking has changed.
materials: optional: question prompt cards to spark ideas
learning_objectives: Develop the habit of noticing philosophical questions in everyday life
Distinguish between questions with factual answers and questions requiring reasoning
Value wonder and curiosity as intellectual virtues
variations: Categorize questions by type (ethical, metaphysical, epistemological)
Vote on which question to discuss next
Create a class book of big questions with drawings

name: Argument Building Blocks -- Constructing Reasons
description: Using physical cards or digital tools, students construct arguments by combining premise cards and conclusion cards. Start simple: 'All dogs are animals. Spot is a dog. Therefore, Spot is an animal.' Advance to evaluating whether arguments are valid (the logic works) and sound (the premises are actually true). Students build, evaluate, and challenge each other's arguments.
materials: premise and conclusion cards (pre-made or student-created)
learning_objectives: Understand the structure of an argument: premises and conclusion
Distinguish between valid and invalid argument forms
Evaluate whether an argument is sound (valid with true premises)
variations: Create arguments about topics students care about
Intentionally build flawed arguments for others to critique
Map arguments from news articles or speeches

name: Fallacy Detective Game -- Spotting Weak Reasoning
description: Students become 'Fallacy Detectives' who identify logical fallacies in real-world examples: advertisements, political speeches, social media posts, movie dialogue, and everyday conversation. Each fallacy has a name, a description, and examples. Students compete to find the most fallacies in a set of materials, then create their own fallacious arguments for others to detect.
materials: fallacy reference cards (ad hominem, straw man, false dilemma, appeal to authority, circular reasoning, red herring, slippery slope)
collection of real-world examples containing fallacies
learning_objectives: Identify common logical fallacies in everyday reasoning
Understand why fallacies are persuasive even though they are logically weak
Apply fallacy detection to media, conversation, and own thinking
variations: Create fallacy-filled advertisements
Analyze a political debate for fallacies
Keep a 'fallacy journal' for a week

name: The Fairness Challenge -- When Equal Isn't Fair
description: Present scenarios where fairness is complicated: Three children want cake but one is allergic. Two students did a project but one did more work. A race where one runner starts further back. Is it fair to give everyone the same thing? Is it fair to give people what they need? What is the difference between equality and equity? Discuss, disagree, and reason together.
materials: optional: physical objects to divide (blocks, stickers) to make scenarios concrete
learning_objectives: Recognize that fairness involves complex reasoning, not simple rules
Distinguish between equality (same treatment) and equity (proportional treatment)
Practice giving moral reasons for positions, not just stating preferences
variations: Create original fairness dilemmas
Role-play different perspectives in a fairness dispute
Connect to real-world issues: school rules, resource distribution, justice

name: Ethical Dilemma Discussions -- When Values Collide
description: Present genuine ethical dilemmas where important values conflict: Should you tell the truth if it hurts someone? Is it right to break a law to help someone? Should one person sacrifice for the many? Students discuss using three ethical lenses: consequences (what leads to the best outcome?), duty (what rule should everyone follow?), and character (what would a virtuous person do?). The goal is not to reach agreement but to reason well.
materials: ethical framework reference cards (consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics)
learning_objectives: Apply multiple ethical frameworks to the same dilemma
Recognize that ethical reasoning involves genuine difficulty, not obvious answers
Develop the ability to disagree respectfully while giving reasons
variations: Students write their own dilemmas
Apply frameworks to current events
Compare how different cultures approach the same ethical question

name: How Do You Know? Evidence Game -- Tracing Knowledge to Its Source
description: Make a claim: 'The Earth goes around the Sun.' Then trace the evidence chain: How do you know? Did you see it? Did someone tell you? Did you read it? How do they know? Keep asking 'How do you know?' until you reach the foundation of the claim. Discover that some knowledge rests on direct experience, some on authority, some on reasoning, and some on trust. Discuss when each source is reliable.
materials: discussion prompts about reliability of sources
learning_objectives: Understand that knowledge claims have sources that can be evaluated
Trace the evidence chain from claim to foundation
Recognize different types of evidence (perception, testimony, reasoning, authority)
variations: Trace knowledge chains for everyday beliefs
Compare reliable and unreliable sources
Discuss what makes testimony trustworthy

name: Optical Illusions & Reliable Senses -- Can You Trust What You See?
description: Explore optical illusions that demonstrate the limits of perception: the Mueller-Lyer illusion, the Ames room, color constancy illusions, motion illusions. Discuss: If our senses can trick us, how do we know when to trust them? Does this mean we can never trust our eyes? What is the difference between perception being unreliable sometimes and being unreliable always?
materials: measuring tools (ruler, string) to verify reality
discussion prompts about perception and knowledge
learning_objectives: Understand that perception is constructed by the brain, not passively received
Recognize situations where senses are unreliable
Reason about when sensory evidence is trustworthy and when it requires verification
variations: Create your own optical illusions
Explore illusions in other senses (sound, touch)
Connect to epistemology: what does this mean for knowledge?

name: Beautiful vs. Pretty: Art Discussion -- Developing Aesthetic Judgment
description: Look at diverse artworks: classical paintings, abstract art, nature photography, street art, a blank canvas, a crumpled piece of paper. For each, discuss: Is this beautiful? Is this art? What is the difference between beautiful and pretty? Can something be ugly and still be good art? Who decides what counts as art? Give reasons for your judgments, not just reactions.
materials: aesthetic judgment worksheet (what I see, what I think, what I feel, why)
learning_objectives: Develop the ability to give reasons for aesthetic judgments
Distinguish between personal preference and reasoned aesthetic evaluation
Engage with the philosophical question of what makes something art
variations: Include non-visual art forms (music, poetry, dance)
Explore aesthetic traditions from different cultures
Create art and defend its aesthetic value

name: What Makes a Good Story? -- Philosophy Through Narrative
description: Read or tell a story, then discuss its philosophical dimensions: What makes this a good story? Is it the plot, the characters, the meaning, the way it makes you feel? What does the story teach about life, identity, or what matters? Every great story is a philosophical thought experiment. Explore themes of purpose, identity, mortality, love, justice, and meaning through narrative.
materials: story or picture book with philosophical themes
discussion prompts connecting narrative to philosophy
optional: writing materials for student responses
learning_objectives: Recognize philosophical themes in narratives and stories
Use stories as vehicles for exploring questions of meaning and purpose
Develop the ability to interpret narrative beyond surface-level plot
variations: Students write their own philosophical stories
Compare how different stories address the same philosophical theme
Discuss: can a story be true even if it is fictional?

name: The Consciousness Question -- Do Machines Think?
description: Explore thought experiments about consciousness: Could a computer ever truly think? (Turing Test) Could a person who speaks no Chinese fool you into thinking they understand Chinese by following rules? (Chinese Room) Is there something it is like to be a bat? What makes something conscious? Discuss: What is the difference between acting intelligent and being intelligent? What would convince you that a machine is conscious?
materials: thought experiment descriptions (Turing Test, Chinese Room, Mary's Room, philosophical zombies)
optional: interact with an AI chatbot and discuss whether it is conscious
learning_objectives: Engage with the philosophical problem of consciousness and mind
Understand major thought experiments about minds and machines
Reason about the difference between behavior and inner experience
variations: Design your own test for consciousness
Debate: will AI ever be conscious?
Explore animal consciousness: which animals are conscious and how do we know?

name: Socratic Seminar Practice -- Facilitating Philosophical Dialogue
description: Students take turns facilitating a Socratic seminar. The facilitator selects a philosophical text or question, prepares opening questions, and guides the group through inquiry without lecturing or giving answers. The rest of the group practices the skills of philosophical dialogue: listening carefully, building on ideas, challenging respectfully, admitting uncertainty, and changing their minds when convinced.
materials: Socratic seminar guidelines (facilitator and participant roles)
learning_objectives: Practice facilitating philosophical inquiry without providing answers
Develop active listening and idea-building skills in group discussion
Learn to challenge ideas respectfully while remaining open to being convinced
variations: Inner/outer circle format (fishbowl)
Text-based seminar vs. question-based seminar
Video-record and self-evaluate facilitation skills
